content:
Submission Deadline: May 15, 2026
Recent advances in AI have been driven by rapid scaling of data, models, and compute—but at growing energy, resource, and environmental costs that few labs can afford. This workshop focuses on the sustainability of AI systems across their lifecycle, emphasizing data-, compute-, and energy-efficient methods, as well as quantitative, reproducible metrics and benchmarks.
We invite contributions on methods, metrics, and real-world applications that enable AI systems to be both powerful and sustainable.
Topics of Interest Data- and label-efficient learning under resource constraints, including theoretical analyses and empirical studies Leveraging underutilized or unconventional data sources to reduce data and compute demands High-performance computing ML pipelines, including energy-, resource-, hardware-, and time-efficient perception and inference Efficient multimodal AI (e.g., adaptive modality selection, sensor scheduling, shared representations) Sustainable re-use, adaptation, and life-cycle management of ML models: fine-tuning of foundation models, in-context and test-time learning, continual learning, federated learning Metrics, methodologies, and tools for measuring efficiency and sustainability (budgeted performance, carbon accounting, standardized reporting, benchmarks) Edge and embedded deployments: design, deployment, and evaluation of efficient models in industrial, agricultural, mobility, healthcare, and other real-world environments Sustainable hardware and materials: algorithm–hardware co-design, low-power accelerators, environmentally responsible hardware life-cycles Ethical, socio-economic, and socio-cultural aspects of sustainable AI: equity of access, environmental justice, governance, and policy implications Submission Opens Soon Submission Tracks Full Papers (Archival — PMLR Proceedings) Up to 7 pages for main content + up to 2 pages for references (9 pages total) Accepted papers will be published in a dedicated volume of the Proceedings of Machine Learning Research (PMLR) Criteria: novelty, technical soundness, potential impact, clarity Short Papers (Non-Archival) Up to 4 pages + 1 page for references (5 pages total) Intended for work-in-progress, preliminary results, and ideas benefiting from feedback Non-archival: authors may later submit extended versions elsewhere Position Papers (Non-Archival) Up to 2 pages + 1 page for references (3 pages total) For research visions, open challenges, benchmark proposals, or critical perspectives Primarily serve as discussion starters at the workshop All submissions must follow the official IJCAI workshop formatting guidelines and will undergo double-blind peer review.
Important Dates All deadlines are Anywhere on Earth (AoE). Milestone Date Submission deadline (all tracks) May 15, 2026 Notification of acceptance June 1, 2026 Camera-ready deadline June 15, 2026
summary:

We invite contributions on methods, metrics, and real-world applications that enable AI systems to be both powerful and sustainable.

title: Organizing Committee

content:
The workshop is a half-day event combining invited keynotes, contributed paper presentations, and interactive discussion rounds.
TimeSession ~30 min Keynote I
Prof. Dr. Paul Lukowicz — DFKI, Germany
Title TBD ~60 min Paper Presentations — Session I ~45 min Position Paper Discussion Round
Floating group discussions per position paper topic, followed by brief summaries ~30 min Keynote II
Speaker TBD
Title TBD ~10 min Best Paper Award & Closing Remarks The workshop shares coffee breaks and lunch with the main conference to encourage cross-workshop interaction and informal networking. Exact break times are set by the IJCAI organizers.
